notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Since tax and health savings matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Given what I know about you — self-employed as a UX consultant, purchasing individual marketplace health insurance, and preferring low-admin financial systems — your HSA question is particularly relevant. Here's a structured look at what matters most for your situation.
Eligibility: the one gate that controls everything
You can only contribute to an HSA in any month when you're enrolled in an HSA-eligible health plan. For 2026, that includes:
A High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) with at minimum a $1,700 deductible (self-only) or $3,400 (family), and an out-of-pocket maximum no higher than $8,500 (self-only) or $17,000 (family)
A Bronze ACA marketplace plan — starting in 2026, all Bronze plans are treated as HSA-compatible even if they don't technically meet the HDHP deductible thresholds
A Catastrophic ACA plan, if you qualify for one
Since you're buying individual marketplace coverage, there's a good chance you're on a Bronze or Catastrophic plan already. If so, you're likely eligible to contribute in 2026 — worth confirming on your plan documents or with your insurer.
You'd lose eligibility for any month you have disqualifying "other coverage," including Medicare enrollment or being covered under a non-HSA-compatible plan as a dependent.
2026 contribution limits
The numbers for this year:
Self-only coverage: $4,400 maximum (you + any employer contribution combined)
Family coverage: $8,750 maximum
Catch-up (age 55+): an additional $1,000 per eligible spouse
Since you're self-employed with no employer, the full $4,400 (assuming self-only) is yours to contribute directly. As a self-employed filer, you contribute directly to the HSA rather than through payroll — the deduction is still "above-the-line" on your 1040, meaning you get the tax benefit even when taking the standard deduction.
Why the HSA is particularly compelling for your situation
The HSA offers what's often called a triple tax advantage — no other common account matches all three:
Contributions are tax-deductible (reduces your Schedule C net income or AGI)
Growth inside the account is tax-free
Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free
For a self-employed consultant with uneven income, this matters in a specific way: in a high-revenue year, maximizing your HSA contribution reduces taxable income immediately — similar to a SEP IRA contribution, but with more flexibility in how you use the funds. In a lower-income year, you can simply contribute less or nothing, since there's no requirement to contribute every year.
After age 65, HSA funds can also be used for non-medical expenses (taxed like a traditional IRA withdrawal, no penalty) — so there's no downside to building the balance.
Key checks before contributing
Confirm your plan is HSA-eligible — check your insurance card or Summary of Benefits for "HSA-compatible" or HDHP language. Bronze ACA plans qualify automatically in 2026.
No disqualifying coverage — if you were briefly on a non-HDHP plan earlier this year, your contribution may need to be prorated. Eligibility is determined month-by-month based on your coverage on the first day of each month.
Telehealth: A 2026-permanent rule allows HSA-eligible plans to cover telehealth before the deductible without disqualifying you — worth knowing if your plan has this feature.
Practical priority order for your dollars
Given your self-employed, low-admin preference, a sensible sequence typically looks like:
Emergency fund covering your deductible + out-of-pocket max (since as a sole proprietor, a bad medical year has no employer buffer)
Max HSA ($4,400 if self-only) — highest tax efficiency per dollar for your situation
SEP IRA or Solo 401(k) contributions for additional retirement savings
Would you like to walk through how your HSA contributions interact with your estimated quarterly tax payments or self-employment income?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
